old_claim.note: PAM Specificity Engineering is modeled as a necessary component or method for this technology in the current graph.

new_claim.note: CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing requires a target DNA sequence with a compatible PAM because Cas9 DNA recognition and cleavage strictly require PAM.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract describing Cas9 DNA recognition and cleavage, canonical 5'-NGG-3' PAM, and PAM-dependent target DNA melting.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The paper reports a Cas9-sgRNA-target-DNA structure and states that RNA-guided DNA recognition and cleavage strictly require a PAM in the target DNA.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports a required mechanism edge because it identifies PAM presence as part of the Cas9 target-recognition architecture, not merely as an optimization or later variant.

ontology_before: The graph treated engineered PAM specificity as a hard prerequisite for ordinary CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing, mixing a later optimization technology with the basic PAM compatibility constraint.
ontology_after: The graph now uses a separate PAM-recognition constraint as the hard prerequisite for CRISPR-Cas9 editing, while engineered PAM-specificity variants are modeled as a later technology built on CRISPR-Cas9, PAM recognition, and protein engineering.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant preserved: CRISPR-Cas9 editing still has a hard PAM dependency. The prerequisite node changed from later engineered variants to the basic Cas9 PAM-recognition constraint.
would_reject_if: A source showed ordinary CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing can occur without any compatible PAM or PAM-equivalent target-recognition constraint.
The corrected graph still made engineered PAM-specificity variants a prerequisite for the 2013 CRISPR-Cas9 genome-editing node.
The new PAM-recognition node depended on later engineered Cas9 variants or otherwise reintroduced the same time reversal.
short_reason: Ordinary CRISPR-Cas9 editing requires PAM compatibility, but engineered PAM-specificity variants are later optimization technologies rather than prerequisites.
